title: Ghost Datacenters and Abundant Watts: Huang's Argument That China Already Has Enough Compute

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
Jensen Huang makes a provocative and detailed case that China already possesses sufficient compute capacity to train and deploy frontier AI models, rendering export controls largely ineffective at their stated goal. His argument rests on several pillars: China has abundant energy (including "ghost datacenters" that are fully powered but empty), massive semiconductor manufacturing capacity at 7nm (which he equates to Hopper-class performance), demonstrated silicon photonics for interconnecting chips at scale, and approximately half the world's AI researchers.

The energy argument is particularly counterintuitive. Huang points out that AI is fundamentally a parallel computing problem. If energy is cheap and abundant, you can compensate for less advanced chips by simply using more of them. The US, ironically, is energy-constrained — which is why Nvidia must relentlessly optimize performance per watt. China faces no such constraint. Huang argues this asymmetry means that even with inferior process nodes, China can aggregate compute to match or exceed what's needed for frontier capabilities.

Huang directly challenges the premise that the flop gap between US and Chinese compute is decisive. He notes that Huawei just had the largest revenue year in its history, shipping millions of AI chips. He argues that 7nm chips are "essentially Hopper" in capability, and that today's frontier models were largely trained on Hopper-generation hardware. The suggestion that China is somehow stuck with inadequate compute, in his view, fundamentally misunderstands the scale of their manufacturing and energy infrastructure.

Perhaps most striking is his claim about algorithmic leverage. Huang notes that Moore's Law delivers roughly 25% annual improvement, but algorithmic advances — like mixture-of-experts architectures or novel attention mechanisms — can yield 10x gains. Since China has an enormous pool of talented AI researchers, their ability to innovate algorithmically means raw hardware gaps matter less than critics assume. DeepSeek, he argues, is direct evidence of this dynamic in action.

The net implication is uncomfortable for export control advocates: the compute threshold for dangerous AI capabilities has likely already been crossed in China. Huang's position is that the remaining policy question isn't whether China can build frontier AI — it's whether the world's AI ecosystem develops on American or non-American technology stacks.
